item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Wonderful. You've essentially captured the essence of one of coworkers and made him a disembodied voice with more self awareness. The frustration you describe is spot on as well.

item.title: +1 for me
item.content:encoded:
When i saw the title, i was thinking of a cognitohazard or something.
But this-
Hoo boy. The ending, my gods! You always make good endings to your scips!
Another +1 from me, siree.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: That last line sealed the deal for me, this is fantastic.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
very interesting character piece, and the ending is very nice

this makes the cliche of "something trapped in an anomaly" feel fresh again

1+

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
The foundation now knows what this SCP wants. Future convo may go much more smoothly if this can be used, either positively or negatively.

Is the screaming rocks another SCP? How is the friend still alive in there…?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Thanks for the read! I agree. Some of my favorite SCPs are the ones who push the "object class" definition: Keter objects that are totally harmless, Safe class that are dangerous but easily contained, etc.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This is a fantastic SCP! It's a well-written and intriguing concept, and the interview logs help show that even for mundane SCPs that don't pose any danger, the Foundation can still have a hard time keeping them under control and getting them to do what they want.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
To be honest, I wasn't even considering rival GoIs.

Admittedly, it's more an unspoken in-universe issue, but when stationary SCP's that cannot be practically contained are concerned, especially ones in public areas, I figure espionage rules apply. "Always assume the enemy is watching".

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
They're posing as security staff at the casino who happen to be keeping an eye on that area.

That'd work much better.

To be honest, I wasn't even considering rival GoIs. Unless their existence is referenced within an article (individually, or mentioning the presense of GoIs in general), I can't automatically assume any one aspect of the wider mythos is in play; it didn't register as a potential complication.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I would change "the entrance" to "the room containing",

That's an oversight on my part after I revised the actual SCP designation to refer to the stall, not the bathroom proper. Originally there was an AOE effect that suppressed video and audio technology around the stall, so the entire bathroom was "anomalous". I've since changed that, but this got overlooked. Good catch!

Speaking of conspicuous, it'd be really out of the ordinary for two guards…

The real concern isn't random passerbys, but other GOIs. Who knows what, say, the GOC might do. "He knows too much! Toss a grenade into the stall!" He's already demonstrated knowledge on blood rituals, what if a Fifthist group was aware of it? Just because the Foundation doesn't know how to effectively extract knowledge from SCP-4281-1 doesn't mean it's not still dangerously insightful. A rotating shift of guards seemed like a reasonable precaution. That said, I'm seriously considering adding in a line that they're not just chillin' by the bathroom. They're posing as security staff at the casino who happen to be keeping an eye on that area.

More than anything, I didn't want to pull focus away from the core story. I could have gone into how the Foundation approached the casino to keep the bathroom closed, how the security officers were introduced into the staff, and other side-content like how the SCP was first discovered, etc, but more explanation and information doesn't always make a better article.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
The entrance to SCP-4281 should remain locked and marked with an "out of service" sign.

I would change "the entrance" to "the room containing", as the anomaly itself has a door/entrance, so this could be misconstrued as requiring two guards to stand around inside a bathroom all day, which would be ridiculously conspicuous.

Speaking of conspicuous, it'd be really out of the ordinary for two guards to be standing outside an out of service restroom to begin with. It also seems to be a waste of resources. Couldn't they simply lock the door? I'm sure the casino's own security would be perfectly capable of handling any random passerby trying to break into a bathroom.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Never played Blood Money! Only Hitman 1 & 2. Thanks for the read.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Cheers! I'm glad you enjoyed it.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: You've done a really excellent job here. I particularly liked the tangibility and visual quality of the dialogue during the 'Creepy Cam' section. That's how you really nail getting across a particular pronunciation and cadence through only text alone.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: maybe it's just me but the stall looks kinda like the casino level with the sheik in hitman: blood money

item.title: Wow nice
item.content:encoded: I'm surprised new page and already really high ratings. Good SCP +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Chances are just as good that it'd start discussing how Coke Zero is a sham, and it can totally taste the difference from Classic Coke.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Plenty of safe-level SCPs have guards, or are housed in areas that are guarded. SCP-714 is a good example! Safe class, but valuable for what it can do, and thus requires guarding. Since SCP-4281 is stationary, and in a high-traffic area, a live presence is necessary. Doesn't mean it's any harder to contain, just requiring protection.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: The requirement of guards makes it seem more like a Euclid to me.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
You’ve managed to genuinely make me attached to a cowboy in a bathroom stall. Upvote for that alone.

On another note, I can’t help but wonder if SCP-4281-1 would be able to divulge information about other secretive government areas, depending if it were talking to the person??

Not explicitly needed by a long shot, just food for thought.

item.title: Re: Nice
item.content:encoded:
Thank you, that makes editing much smoother.

Some other user told me the wiki lacked such a function, but I'm relieved that's not the case.

Also no problem to T Rutherford.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Hey Hippo. Nope, I responded to your comment too lol. You can see it below. You were both just making similar points about the anomalous effects preventing a full view of SCP-4281-1's appearance.

Honestly, out of your suggestions, the very last one has the most appeal to me. In fact, I'd reduce the "walking in, walking out" element, and just make the stall accessible. There's just nothing inside. When the stall is open, the feet vanish, and SCP-4281-1 is nowhere to be found.

EDIT: Article revised! Thanks again for your input!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I'm guessing you replied to the wrong comment (pardon in advance if that's not the case!), so:

There's a lot of weird, simple solutions you can use here. One of the things to keep in mind is that your solution doesn't have to plug every "hole"; it just has to be interesting, brief, and fun enough for us to (mostly) not care. For example:

+ show block
– hide block
  • Describe any attempt to approach the stall from above or otherwise intrude in its space as suffering from Zeno's dichotomy paradox. You can only cover half the distance with each attempt (both people and machines perpetually stop half way, thinking they've arrived, only to "realize" they've only traveled half the distance).
  • Just make him a cognitohazard that people can't describe (even when looking at a picture). If you want to elaborate on this, refer to visual recognition bots describing him as something silly ("duck with squirrel on head").
  • Have every attempt to look into his stall result in person or machine looking in the wrong stall. Make how this works vague and confusing (implying the Foundation doesn't understand it themselves).
  • Everyone and everything that looks inside the stall proceeds to spontaneuosly combust. Make a brief humorous aside/implication about the Foundation discovering that this includes things literally incapable of combusting.
  • The stall is literally empty. You can only see his feet from underneath it; you're "peeking into" a completely different space. Attempts to explore this space via endoscope or crawling through result in you entering another stall (or even better, you just "come out" the same way you came in). Again, you can keep this vague and confusing.

I'm fond of the Xeno solution, if only because I think it emphasizes the futility of the skip (reflected in their inability to get useful information, and his inability to make a human connection). I'd skip the drones and just mention the problem occurs with attempts at endoscopic exploration (because endoscopic exploration of a bathroom stall is both incredibly pervy but also very logical and something the Foundation would probably do).

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Thanks! I owe the subversion concept to my critters, honestly. They gave me the idea. My original concept was purely malicious, came off as very bland, and lacked a strong ending hook.

As for the "EXPUNGED vs REDACTED" issue, I did try to remain consistent with Foundation lore when using it. According to: http://www.scp-wiki.net/forum/t-229758/expunged-versus-redacted

"[DATA EXPUNGED]" when (in-universe) removing classified data — stuff that the reader doesn't have clearance to see. "[DATA REDACTED]" will just mean that something has been removed for clarity or appropriateness of expression"

Which seemed fairly logical to me. What the security officer said would have been deliberately erased by the Foundation archives because of any number of sinister reasons, left ambiguous.

If you, or anyone else has any ideas for means by which SCP-4281-1 remains (mostly) imperceptible, I'd love to hear it! I definitely struggled with finding a credible means of giving SCP-4281-1 it's characteristic "feet, socks and shoes" appearance.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Thanks for the response! If you check the crit, and response from TheMightyMcB above, you'll see I originally had a brief line about the interior of the stall being "indescribable". I agreed that, compounded with the electronics failure and embarrassment effect, it was just throwing too many separate effects at the problem.

At the end of the day, the core of the article isn't the anomalous extra-spacial nature of SCP-4281. It's more of a side-note to enable to quirky visual of SCP-4281-1 being nothing more than calves, socks and sneakers. I wanted to focus more on the story than plugging up out every possible plot hole.

That said, I'd love to hear ideas on possible anomalous means to prevent visual inspection of SCP-4281. Lay it up!

item.title: Re: Nice
item.content:encoded: Thanks! Like th4nkyoub3n mentioned, I can see the changes through Show History - Compare Versions. Good eye catching those typos. I appreciate it.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I think my only serious complaints are that the DATA EXPUNGED should (probably) be DATA REDACTED (expunged means no one is allowed to see it), and that the central anomaly preventing interaction/perception with him is a little too ornate (drones failing, compulsion effects, etc). I feel there's probably a simple, more elegant way to prevent interaction that doesn't rely on these effects.

The interviews are fun, and have a weird charm. This reminds me a lot of SCP-270, actually (a phone that gives just enough sensitive information to keep the Foundation listening), except now the voice has a personality and more character. The final bit is what got me to upvote, since it manages to subvert the expectation of sinister motivations (which you set up with the whole Lawrence thing).

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Two things about this: If it's possible to peek over or under the stall, why isn't SCP-4281's appearance known? There is an embarassment compulsion effect upon doing so, but there aren't any mentions of amnestic properties.

Secondly, this reminds me heavily of SCP-3361 - Classy Carlos Storms The Shitter. Though, I personally believe this to be better-written, and deserving of a +1.

item.title: Re: Nice
item.content:encoded: You can click on "Show History" and then "Compare Versions"

item.title: Nice
item.content:encoded:
I like this SCP. Especially the very last bit of the last test log. He's like a guy who wants to make friends but talks too much to succeed. Maybe he's just overwhelmed by all the stuff he knows.

I made a few grammar edits. Since this wiki apparently lacks a "Show Changes" function, the original lines are below:

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Thank you!

I had considered Euclid, but honestly, the Foundation can only base their containment procedures on what they currently know. Any number of safe-level SCPs could, by virtue of just being "anomalous", be Euclid, but thus far have proven easy to contain.

Yes, SCP-4281-1 is mysterious, and knows a great deal, but as far as the Foundation has seen, it's stationary, and they can easily control who has access to that knowledge with a few guards and a locked door.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This is fantastic- just the right amount of hilarious and unsettling. The dialogue is an absolute riot. I think it should be Euclid rather than Safe, though, given how much it knows and how mysterious it is.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Thank you for the response. I think you really captured my intended ideas with this skip, especially the core message. All anomaly and omniscience aside, It's about a creature trapped in more ways than one, unable to make a connection with the outside world.

P.S. Comparing talking SCP-4281 to being dragged by a truck down a highway is even better than I could have described it. :)

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
This is great.

+ long rant
- long rant

The image is an amazing hook. You're just invited to imagine yourself entering a nondescript bathroom, and then being pulled into a conversation with an unknown figure sitting in the stall.

The dialogue really takes the typical "SCP with interview log" and just throws it out the window. He's not the meek interviewee who just answers plain questions directly, and he's not the monologuer who divulges a long story about the subject at hand. Instead, the poor interviewer is being dragged along like they're tied to the back of a truck going down the highway, trying desperately to turn the conversation to something useful as the guy just goes on and on and on.

The last interview further exemplified this, with a reference to somebody the interviewer cared about, but was let completely unsatisfied as he started rambling about a K-mart. You finish that incident with a statement by the guard, using a redaction to spark the imagination about whether he was threatened, told the bad news, or something else entirely.

And then finally the ending, when the scip realizes he may have a problem.

You can even look at this as metaphorical in a way: the scip is stuck in a bathroom, and like in life, can't really control what people he comes across. Deep down he wants to be understood and connect with other people, but his self-imposed separation prevents that. He both is physically and emotionally isolated, and he doesn't know how to pull himself out.

Well done author. +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Not everyone digs the short form SCP, but I myself am a fan of stuff like that. Stuff like the teleporting party pool, the salsa hammer, and the god of middle aged white guys named steve are some of my favorite articles on the site, and I do think this would work as a short-form as well. That being said, this extended version also works, so no complaints there.

Like you said, can't please everyone. Just figured I'd leave my 2 cents.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Thank you so much for your crit! UPDATE: Grammar and formatting errors corrected!

+ Show Block
- Close

Continuing to feel like a cop-out. How did they look inside if all of the above was true? I would pick one deterrent and stick with it.

I've decided to stick with the embarrassment, coupled with recording failure. Yes, it's a cop-out, but like you said, I'm struggling to find an alternative. In the least, the embarrassment angle feels like a simple amplification, and a play on the absurdity of SCP-4281. After all, you're literally trying to force your way into/peek inside a bathroom stall.

This whole log was fucking amazing, and I loved every minute of it.

Cheers. Dialogue is definitely easier for me than technical writing. I'm still getting the hang of the "clinical" tone of Articles.

This one wasn't as funny as the first one, but I can't see a version of this that doesn't dash your pacing to the rocks without including it. Maybe trim it down slightly?

I had some trouble with this one for sure. I wanted it to be quick and to the point, a good lull between Log 1 and Log 13. Just establish that this person, this thing, isn't looking to escape, we have nothing to offer it, and honestly, it just wants to ramble.

Okay woah, I did not see this coming, and holy shit was it good. I would honestly have upvoted if this was just the first log, not gonna lie. Some crazy cowboy in a bathroom stall that doesn't shut up. But then this hit, and goddamn.

With my previous compliment, I do have a caveat. I'm not a fan of this as the ending. The whole way through that interview, I was thinking that -1 was getting all personal and nasty due to their attempt at being fake to him (lying, etc.), and then this final bit here clashes with that change in temper in a way that I'm not sure I like. I honestly think it'd be better if you ended at "So I said to him- Oh! Uh, well, alright then! Have a good night fellas! Come back any time!"

So, here's the funny part! If you check for "Stalled Conversation" on the Drafts forum, you'll see my original draft was very much in-line with what you were hoping. Originally, there was only one test log, a variation on 1-13, that portrayed SCP-4281-1 as more malicious. It closed with "Y'all come back any time!"

Along with my official crit, no less than three people in chat said they disliked SCP-4281-1 as spiteful or manipulative, and found the farcically tragic angle more compelling. Additionally, Lev (thanks again!) thought the narrative pacing was too quick-to-the-punch with only one test log.

So, eh! What could have been. It's one of those "you can't please everyone" situations, but I'm happy with the ending, and the extended logs.

Y'all have a good day now!

Please collapse long posts ~ Leveritas

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I'd have loved to read how someone outside of the Foundation reacts to the weird stuff the guy is saying. Maybe have someone get really invested in what he's saying and taking notes, eventually figuring things out.

Sorry I'm really tired right now and can't formulate my thoughts right. Good going though! Send me a PM if you write something else, I like your style!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Before I get into my little review here, I should mention that you haven't hosted your image on-site. You should probably do that (it's under the "Files" option at the bottom of the page). Just change the file name in the image block to be what the image is called.

Anyways, let's get to it!

+ Open Crit
- Close Crit

2m2 area of SCP-4281

Assuming you mean that to be "2m2 area around SCP-4281", yeah? Superscripts are made by bookending whatever is getting sent up with "^^", so you'd type in "^^2^^".

Anyone hearing these exclamations will be overwhelmed with a feeling of intense embarrassment.

Not gonna lie, this kind of feels like a cop out. I don't know what else you could do, but "intense embarrassment" feels cheap.

sound-blocking technology or possessing a hearing impediment

I feel like neither of those are the correct technical terms for what they describe. "soundproofing/noise-cancelling" and "hearing impairment" are probably what you're looking for.

Individuals who manage to look inside SCP-4281-1's stall are wholly incapable of describing the interior.

Continuing to feel like a cop-out. How did they look inside if all of the above was true? I would pick one deterrent and stick with it.

phrases such as “That’s right, huh? Of course it is.”, “Another thing about that…”, “And, heck, you know what that reminds me of?”

Missing your "and" to separate the three features of the list. Might wanna start the last quote with "Heck".

the Foundation, and several GOI's

Should be "the Foundation and several GOIs." or "the Foundation, as well as several GOIs."

Dr. Clarke remained in the stall for an additional 3 hours, fruitlessly attempting to guide the conversation back to topics of interest.

This whole log was fucking amazing, and I loved every minute of it.

Test Log - 4281-1-01

Test 05 is titled as Test 01.

Test Log - SCP-4281-1-05

This one wasn't as funny as the first one, but I can't see a version of this that doesn't dash your pacing to the rocks without including it. Maybe trim it down slightly?

Test Log - SCP-4281-1-13

Okay woah, I did not see this coming, and holy shit was it good. I would honestly have upvoted if this was just the first log, not gonna lie. Some crazy cowboy in a bathroom stall that doesn't shut up. But then this hit, and goddamn.

SCP-4281-1: God dang it. Things were going so well. What'd I do wrong? People like talkin' about themselves, right? … I shoulda' just told him the trucker story. Everyone loves that story. Or maybe I shoulda' mentioned-… nah, he would have found that boring. Maybe I coulda'- no, that's just stupid. Dang it. Dang it!

With my previous compliment, I do have a caveat. I'm not a fan of this as the ending. The whole way through that interview, I was thinking that -1 was getting all personal and nasty due to their attempt at being fake to him (lying, etc.), and then this final bit here clashes with that change in temper in a way that I'm not sure I like. I honestly think it'd be better if you ended at "So I said to him- Oh! Uh, well, alright then! Have a good night fellas! Come back any time!"

Overall, this was totally unexpected and honestly really good. I enjoyed myself, and the dialogue was excellent. Take that +1, man. Good stuff.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Thank you! I had some information like this in an earlier draft, part of the "discovery log" wherein SCP-4281-1 let a patron know of an anomalous method of winning at Roulette. What sort of conversations would you have liked?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I really liked it. Although I'd have loved to know what kind of things he'd say to non-foundation personnel. Would he ramble on about their personal lives or spill secrets he really shouldn't ?

item.title: Stalled Conversation
item.content:encoded:
Big Edit: Thank you everyone for 200 votes! Expect more articles next month!

Image source: https://commons.wikimedia.org/wiki/File:Public_restroom_at_an_Iowa_rest_area.jpg

This file is licensed under the Creative Commons Attribution-Share Alike 4.0 International license.

Thanks to Leveritas for his crit. Thanks also to multiple users from #site19 and #thecritters for their help with this. If we traded drafts, let me know, and I'll link you here: Weryllium Deadly Bread

After the positive feedback I received for my tale A Fond Farewell I decided to push forward with one of my SCP ideas, and submit. I hope you enjoy!

P.S. Thank you to Laneous for editing in the module, and correcting the title. My bad!
P.P.S. Thank you to Henzoid for adding tags!

P.P.P.S. After a good deal of response here in the Discussions, I've decided to revise the core anomaly of SCP-4281. Same story, just no "embarrassment effect" or "drone failure" elements, which always felt the weakness of this article. Cheers to The Great Hippo for the suggestions.

► More T Rutherford Articles and Tales ◄
